---
title: "Power Analysis for Linear Mixed Models: What You Need to Know"
format: html
author: "Martin Sjogard"
date: 2025-06-20
categories: [statistics, R, mixed models, power analysis]
---

## Introduction

Power analysis for linear mixed models (LMMs) is crucial when designing experiments involving repeated measures or hierarchical data structures. In this post, we demonstrate how to simulate LMM data and explore the impact of sample size on statistical power — without requiring specialized packages like `simr`.

## Simulate Repeated Measures Data

```{r}
library(lme4)
set.seed(123)

n_subjects <- 30
n_obs <- 5
subject <- factor(rep(1:n_subjects, each = n_obs))
time <- rep(1:n_obs, n_subjects)
intercept <- 2
slope <- 0.5
subject_effect <- rnorm(n_subjects, 0, 1)
y <- intercept + slope * time + rep(subject_effect, each = n_obs) + rnorm(n_subjects * n_obs, 0, 1)

data <- data.frame(subject, time, y)

model <- lmer(y ~ time + (1 | subject), data = data)
summary(model)

simulate_power <- function(n_subjects, n_sim = 100) {
  p_vals <- replicate(n_sim, {
    subject <- factor(rep(1:n_subjects, each = n_obs))
    subject_effect <- rnorm(n_subjects, 0, 1)
    y <- intercept + slope * time + rep(subject_effect, each = n_obs) + rnorm(n_subjects * n_obs, 0, 1)
    df <- data.frame(subject, time, y)
    m <- lmer(y ~ time + (1 | subject), data = df)
    coef <- summary(m)$coefficients
    coef["time", "Pr(>|t|)"]
  })
  mean(p_vals < 0.05)
}

simulate_power(30)  # Estimated power at n = 30 subjects


<!-- rebuild after permissions update -->
